speaker: interviewee
text: --give you that. These are May '39. [GERMAN] What--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the 13th of May.

speaker: interviewee
text: Doesn't say what the ship was doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to enter in at the Southampton--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, and nothing to go through, because he just--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- we, yeah, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't translate your [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: We didn't [INAUDIBLE]. And I will slate this. My name is Ronnie Hess. Today's date is the 23rd of June, 1996. I am conducting an interview with Survivor Philip Freund. The interview is being conducted in Menomonee Falls, which is just north of Milwaukee, Wisconsin, in the United States. And let me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we start, we need to have you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Slating. My name is Ronnie Hess. It is June the 23rd, 1996. The interview is with Survivor Philip Freund. And it's taking place in Menomonee Falls, which is just north of Milwaukee, Wisconsin, in the United States. The language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to begin by asking you to state your name, spell it, and tell us where you were born and when.

speaker: interviewee
text: Philip Freund, F-R-E-U-N-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: Philip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Philip.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell 'Philip.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, P-H-I-L-I-P. Middle initial is S, as in Sigfried. I was born the 5th of June, 1931, in the city of Munich, Bavaria, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little bit about growing up in Germany in, in the 1930s. Can you tell us what some of your earliest memories were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, probably one of my earliest memories was that all of a sudden, we couldn't go to the park. I couldn't ride the streetcar. We couldn't go to the movies. We were on half rations. And food-- so food was scarce. And there was a tremendous amount of anxiety and stress in the home. And then-- I started going to public school. And I was kicked out of there because I was Jewish. I went to a-- to a Jewish school. And that was burned down on Crystal Night in November of 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: These, these early memories that you have, you were four or five?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. I was five, maybe six, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were one of how many children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a sister, a younger sister. She's three years younger, Lisa. Her name now is Avedon, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was-- what was your family like? You were-- you were a family of-- when, when some of your memories took place, you were a family of three?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- well, a family of three. And then, we-- then, when Lisa came, we were a family of four-- my father, my mother, sister, and myself. And we lived in Munich on Elisabethstrasse 19, in a large building. We-- I had-- I shared room with my sister, if I recall. And we had a, a good-sized unit there. We, we rented.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish neighborhood? Not a Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jewish. No, it was an integrated-- well, my grandmother lived a block down and-- on Tengstrasse. And then across the street was my grandmother's brother. He lived across the street. And down the same street, her other brother, [? Willy, ?] lived across the street. And Hugo lived down the street, oh, about maybe 8, 10 blocks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were-- there was-- it was a large family of my-- on my mother's side. There was no one on my father's side left except his brother, who lived in Frankfurt. And why was there no one left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he just had a brother. His, his mother and father died in the 1920s. And I was born in 1931. So I didn't even know those people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you characterize your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Close-knit. My mother was a very caring person, although [LAUGHS] she loved to socialize. And she, you know-- typical German woman who-- she went to her afternoon coffees down at-- in Prinzregentenstrasse. And she would meet with her mother or her friends. And I had a-- we had a governess who took care of us. And we had a maid. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's how we lived. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: A prosperous Jewish family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a prosperous Jewish family. We-- in the summers, we went to Tegernsee, which is a lake south of Munich. They rented a home there. And we were on a lake. And I, I can remember going swimming in the lake and walking up in the mountains and, and doing things. In fact, I can recall a tremendously rainy summer and watching the, the main stream going through the little town where we stayed and watching trees come floating down. So it-- yeah, we had a-- we had a very nice life. My family owned-- my grandfather started a company called Becker and Rokel in Munich in the year 1900, and then took in his two brothers-in-law. And they were-- did printing, printing cards and also placards for advertisements. And there was quite a bit of money there. We were certainly not lacking for anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Becker, B-E-C-K-E-R and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Rokel, R-O with an umlaut K-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father did what? Did he--

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- I'm sorry. My father was the sales manager for the company. He, he worked there. And then-- it's interesting. In 1951, I went back to, to Germany and went-- called them up in Munich and told them who I was. And they said, we'll send the car for you. And I went back to the factory. And when I got to the-- to the plant, the people who knew me as a little boy lined up in the driveway. And I had to walk, walk down the line, shaking everyone's hands and walked into the office. And my father's secretary still worked there. And it just broke her up. I spent the whole day there, walking around the plant, eating with the workers. And they were all very sorry that we-- the family no longer owned the plant.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll come back to that.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: But at the time that you were growing up, what kinds of things did your family do? You, You went away for summer vacations? Or they weren't vacations, they were-- at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. We would-- we would go away. We would go, and we would do things in Munich, as far as I can recall, going downtown. And we would go to Englischer Garten, which is a very large park in the city of Munich. And on Sunday afternoons, we'd listen to the music. And then at the other end of Elisabethstrasse was Warner Kaserne, which is-- it's now a US military base. But I, I watched the German soldiers on maneuvers out there, planes landing. Now that whole area is part of the Olympic village in Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family was Jewish. Were they observant Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. We had a Christmas tree. [LAUGHS] With-- I remember we had candles. My mother always said it was primarily for the-- for the maid and the governess. But I remember getting, getting presents at that time, during Christmas. I had a train-- electric train that, that I played with. We were-- they did belong to synagogue. And the, the rabbi from Munich also emigrated and ended up in New York City and started a congregation in New York City. And my mother and grandmother joined. That same congregation that was originally in Munich is now in New York City. And the Rabbi Baerwald, he did my bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you would say, then, that you were-- you were assimilated Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, absolutely. Absolutely. My father was an officer in the Kaiser's Army in World War I. And he was accepted. And we got along very well with, with-- as far as I could tell, with the people-- with everyone else in Germany. There was no problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you have a sense that they were indeed problems?

speaker: interviewee
text: As soon as Hitler came to power, and they started restricting the movements and what privileges and rights we had. In 1935, they took away our citizenship. So we were fair game. We had no more legal recourse because we were no longer citizens of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any conversations with your mother about perhaps why you couldn't take public transportation, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember any conversations, no. But I, I do remember that all of a sudden, we couldn't get on the streetcar, And we couldn't go to the park. And we couldn't go to the movies. And I remember wanting to, to see some of the movies, but my mother said we couldn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she explain to you what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe so. I just don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You talked about things became very stressful for the family. Can you elaborate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my, my father was still-- at that time-- in, in-- up until 1937, was still working in the-- in the company. And he would travel all over Europe. And he would-- then would bring back food from Sweden. I remember him bringing cheese and butter and eggs from Sweden so that that supplemented our, our food. But if he wouldn't have traveled, I-- we would have really been very hungry. So we really didn't have much food. Anna, the governess, took me to the Catholic church, which was around the corner. And the priest gave-- I remember him giving me-- after the Christmas service, giving me an orange. And I had never seen an orange before. And I had no idea even how to eat the thing. And then Anna showed me that you have to peel it and, and eat it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were really lacking all of a sudden, from living fairly well to living very poorly because of the lack of food. And there were travel restrictions. And then the money was also-- my mother's bank account was [GERMAN]. That means blocked-- so that she couldn't have access to her money without going and getting permission from the Gestapo The fact that you had-- that you had rations-- that you were rationed because you were-- you were Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everyone was rationed, except we had-- we were on half rations. They would give us a half a ration book rather than the whole ration book. I remember that because I, I would go shopping with the maid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she, she said, well, we can only get so much. And I said, well, why are these other people getting twice as much, you know, much more than we are? And she said, well, this is all we have coupons for.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I-- when I got home, my mother told me that we only were also authorized half rations. And there was a big red J marked on our ID cards as well as on the ration book.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- at a very young age, you knew that there was-- that you were, in fact, very different and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Discriminated against, that's right, simply because we were Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about the difficulties that your father may have had traveling around Europe in the 1930s?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I know that he would-- he would come and go. I know that after-- a long time afterwards, my mother told me that he knew Count Bernadotte and-- who wanted him to, to come to Sweden and start up a printing plant there and that he would put up the money. And my father would put up expertise. And my father told him that he couldn't do that because he was German. And he had served in the Kaiser's Army in World War I. And he was not about to, to leave Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Count Bernadotte was--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: A military men or married to royalty or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Married to royalty, yeah. He, he was part of the royalty. I don't-- he was probably married to royalty. But he also came out of the royal house himself. And then he was-- he-- he'd saved from Jews in World War-- you know, during-- before and during World War II. And then he-- also, he then died in, in an explosion in, in the King David Hotel in Jerusalem in 1948, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Talk a little, if you-- if you could about your school experiences. You went to a public school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were about how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have been six, six, six years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is-- this is 1937?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I started school there. And then things, things didn't-- all of a sudden got very bad. Kids started beating up on us. There were other Jewish kids also in the school. And the teacher was not very kind. I remember that. And there was a lot of stress. Then my father died. And that didn't help the situation. And then I stayed in that school until November of 1938. But I had a difficult time with the lessons because of the stress and, I guess, lack of food and, and because the other kids really were very unkind to, to all the Jewish kids-- not just to myself, but to all the other ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would it be like? When-- I mean, you were-- you were physically beaten by--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm, physically beaten, spit upon, called 'dirty Jew,' ink thrown on my-- I was learning how to write and read. Ink thrown on, on, on my books-- it was just nasty kids, you know. On the playground, they'd, they'd kick you. They'd, they'd throw things at you. On the way to school, they pushed you into the gutter, that type of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at school, you said that there was not a great deal of support from your teacher. What would the teacher do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. He'd laugh. He thought it was funny. If I remember that-- I remember him standing there. I mean, he was quite a tall man. And I complained that somebody had poured ink on my-- on my writing paper. Because I was copying the letters from the blackboard in this German script, and I had a difficult time. The kid next to me was constantly bothering me. And then he'd poured ink on my paper. And I complained to the teacher. And the teacher did nothing. He thought it was funny.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would your parents say when you would tell them you were having these difficulties at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my, my, my mother-- I, I told the, the, the governess, Anna. And she says, well, there's not much we can do at this point. We have to just keep quiet. She felt that it was better just to take it. And I thought, gee, I can't go through life like this. This is-- this doesn't seem right. I remember thinking about that, that it wasn't-- that it was not good. It was not a good situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you stop going to that school because you couldn't go to that school anymore?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once Crystal Night-- November, 1938, Crystal Night took place. And then they-- all Jews had to leave the schools. And then the synagogue had set up-- set up a school itself. No, I guess it was before that. Because Crystal Night-- at Crystal Night, they burned the synagogue down. And that's when all schools for Jews ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I must've been-- I missed have started that in September of, of '38. Because, as I say, I got kicked out of the regular public school. I just was there a year. Do you remember Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, oh, vividly, very vividly. All of a sudden, there was a, a knock or a ring on the doorbell. I went to answer it. And there was a very large Gestapo man there, SS man. And he asked for my father. Well, my father had-- was already deceased. And I told him my father was dead. He had been dead a little over a year. And he proceeded to kick me and punch me. And I, I went flying up against the wall. And he was about to stomp on me with his boot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother came out-- and heard the noise, came out, and said, what's going on? And the Gestapo man said, I'm looking for Max Freund. And she told him that he was already dead and to, to leave me alone. And then he left. She produced the death certificate to prove that he was really dead. Why were they looking for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: To put all Jews in concentration camps. They put thousands of them in Dachau, which was outside of Munich. My uncles all were locked up. Anyone over the age of, I believe, 16 or 18 went into the concentration camp. And my mother called her girlfriend, who had an automobile. And she took us out to my father's platoon sergeant from World War I. And he hid us out at his farm for a week. That-- it must have been tremendously dangerous for him to hide us out. And my mother, grandmother, and sister stayed in the farmhouse. But I went out in a farm field with the farmer. And I remember he gave me a big hat. And I could wear the big hat. It was a big, floppy hat. And we collected-- he had two bulls pulling the wagon. And I sat up with him. And we'd stop it once in a while. And we'd throw these big sugar beets in the back of the wagon. And then he proceeded to tell me about World War I and my father, and what they did, and that type of thing. My father was in the army from 1916 to 1919.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did he have such vivid memories of your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was his platoon leader. They were in direct contact all day long, every day, the whole time. And he told me like my father. My father, he said, was a-- was a good soldier. He did his best for his company, or his platoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your father die?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] My father tried to sneak money out to Holland. And the Gestapo found out about it and called my mother to Gestapo headquarters. And somehow, they, they got in touch with my father at the hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father and mother-- your father was on a business trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And he was just he was sneaking money out. It was-- it was a capital offense to take money out of the country without the, the authorization. And don't forget, we were no longer citizens.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they picked up my mother and held her in detention-- In Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munich, at the Gestapo headquarters. Contacted my father, told him that they knew that he had violated the laws of-- the currency laws by sneaking money out. And they had my mother in, in the-- in the headquarters. And he then committed suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was a matter of either that he-- well, he felt that was only a way out. He wanted to protect my mother. And he told them that-- evidently, that he was going to take care of the situation. And that's how he took care of it. My mother was released. And he was brought back and then was buried in Munich

speaker: interviewer
text: So, so your father committed suicide in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Amsterdam, in a hotel. I believe he slit his wrists.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was trying to secrete money out of the country because--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I, I think by this time, he came to the realization that it was going to be necessary to leave at some time in the future. And he wanted to make sure that there was sufficient funds to take care of his family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea how they knew that he was doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My cousin, Hans [? Eichenholdt, ?] was-- knew-- but knew the situation, you know. What he did, he took it out of the company business. Because he couldn't take it out of his own personal account because that was blocked. But he took it from the company, I guess, with the idea that he was going to pay them back. And somehow, they did an audit on the books. The government-- the Nazi government came in and did it on the books and found out that there was money missing. And I guess they put two and two together that here, my father was gone-- had, had traveled and took the money with him. Because this was-- I assume this was not the first time he had done that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what does a family do-- a family with two young children, a young mother, do in this kind of a-- in this kind of a world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was very difficult. First of all, losing father and husband was terribly tragic for my mother. My mother, at that time, was 31 years old, two children. There was no more money coming in because he no longer was employed. I, I assume he had some insurance. And they-- my mother lived off of the savings. And then, when Crystal Night came, then she decided that she was going to leave. But I-- it was very difficult. They had-- there was no money coming in.

speaker: interviewer
text: You spent the week in the country. Then you came back to Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: At Crystal Night, yes. Yes, we came back to Munich to our apartment. And that's when my mother decided that it was time for us to, to leave Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how she went about planning that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she had heard that there was a ship that was leaving and that that ship was going to Havana, Cuba, and that you could purchase entry visas for Cuba. She had already applied for coming to this country. Her twin sister was already here with her husband, who was a doctor. And she made plans. She-- by, by that time, she already had sent a lot of things to her sister in New Jersey. A big box-- I remember a huge box of furniture and belongings when she-- at that time, she could still send it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she sent that out because they had-- they came to this country. And he had just-- he was a doctor and didn't have too much. So she sent that to him so that they could have some furnishings.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then in-- by 1938, she had heard about this ship. She had heard about the visas to Cuba. And we were going to wait in Havana until our immigration quota number came up. And then we were going to leave to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so your, your recollections of preparing to leave Germany were what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I really-- I, I remember them doing a lot of packing. There was a lot of packing for this huge box that was-- that was leaving. And then we were left with very little-- and then, as we were about to leave, we packed up two suitcases. That's all that we could take at that time. And we had 10 marks, which was about $4.20. We could leave with that. So we left a lot of things behind, including our maid and governess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do Do you remember leaving your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember we left in the evening and went to the train station. And we boarded this, this train to go to Hamburg. And we got there the next day early in the morning. And my mother got us into this hotel. Now, you got to realize that most, most hotels would not take Jews. But my mother knew of a commercial hotel, like a businessman's hotel, where she could go because they knew my father there. And they allowed us to stay there one night until we could get on the so that we could-- we could leave them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the train ride in terms of any rowdyism on the train, a sense of what was-- did you feel safe on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, didn't feel safe on the train because we were still in Germany. There was no rowdyism. But there was a, a tremendous amount of apprehension because we didn't know if we were getting-- if we were going to get on this ship to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were quite concerned about that. And my mother was very much concerned, my grandmother, also. I mean, both of them were very apprehensive and very-- it was a very stressful time for both-- for, for both those two ladies, as well as for my sister and myself. And we felt very much estranged from the whole world, except for our, our closest immediate relatives. Do you remember the name of the hotel, by the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. I know it was somewhere on the docks, close to the docks. And it wasn't a very nice hotel. Because I remember my mother told me not to take off my clothes. We had to-- we had to sleep with our clothes on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a good time to maybe change tapes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] How's it going?
